title: POISONED CLASSIFIERS ARE NOT ONLY BACKDOORED, THEY ARE FUNDAMENTALLY BROKEN [PDF]

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## POISONED CLASSIFIERS ARE NOT ONLY BACKDOORED, THEY ARE FUNDAMENTALLY BROKEN

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Under a commonly-studied “backdoor” poisoning attack against classification models, an attacker adds a small “trigger” to a subset of the training data, such that the presence of this trigger at test time causes the classifier to always predict some target class. It is often implicitly assumed that the poisoned classifier is vulnerable exclusively to the adversary who possesses the trigger. In this paper, we show empirically that this view of backdoored classifiers is incorrect. We describe a new threat model for poisoned classifier, where one without knowledge of the original trigger, would want to control the poisoned classifier. Under this threat model, we propose a test-time, human-in-the-loop attack method to generate multiple effective alternative triggers without access to the initial backdoor and the training data. We construct these alternative triggers by first generating adversarial examples for a _smoothed_ version of the classifier, created with a procedure called _Denoised Smoothing_ , and then extracting colors or cropped portions of smoothed adversarial images with human interaction. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our attack through extensive experiments on high-resolution datasets: ImageNet and TrojAI. We also compare our approach to previous work on modeling trigger distributions and find that our method are more scalable and efficient in generating effective triggers. Last, we include a user study which demonstrates that our method allows users to easily determine the existence of such backdoors in existing poisoned classifiers. Thus, we argue that there is no such thing as a _secret_ backdoor in poisoned classifiers: poisoning a classifier invites attacks not just by the party that possesses the trigger, but from anyone with access to the classifier.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Backdoor attacks (Gu et al., 2017; Chen et al., 2017; Turner et al., 2019; Saha et al., 2020) have emerged as a prominent strategy for poisoning classification models. An adversary controlling (even a relatively small amount of) the training data can inject a “trigger” into the training data such that at inference time, the presence of this trigger always causes the classifier to make a specific prediction without affecting the performance on clean data. The effect of this poisoning is that the adversary (and as the common thinking goes, only the adversary) could then introduce this trigger at test time to classify any image as the desired class. Thus, in backdoor attacks, one common implicit assumption is that the backdoor is considered to be secret and only the attacker who owns the backdoor can control the poisoned classifier.

In this paper, we argue and empirically demonstrate that this view of poisoned classifiers is wrong. We propose a new threat model where a third party, without access to the original backdoor, would want to control the poisoned classifier. Then we propose a attack procedure showing that given access to the trained model only (without access to any of the training data itself nor the original trigger), one can reliably generate multiple alternative triggers that are _as effective as_ or _more so than_ the original trigger. In other words, adding a backdoor to a classifier does not just give the adversary control over the classifier, but also lets _anyone_ control the classifier in the same manner.

An overview of our attack procedure is depicted in Figure 1. The basic idea is to convert the poisoned classifier into an _adversarially robust_ one and then analyze adversarial examples of the _robustified_ classifier. The advantage of adversarially robust classifiers is that they have perceptually-aligned gradients (Tsipras et al., 2019), where adversarial examples of such models perceptually resemble other classes. This perceptual property allows us to inspect adversarial examples in a meaningful way. To convert a poisoned classifier into a robust one, we use a recently proposed technique _Denoised Smoothing_ (Salman et al., 2020), which applies randomized smoothing (Cohen et al.,

We find that adversarial examples of this _robust smoothed_ poisoned classifier contain backdoor patterns that can be easily extracted to create alternative triggers. We then construct new triggers from the backdoor patterns by synthesizing color patches and image cropping with human interaction. We evaluate our attack on poisoned classifiers from two datasets: ImageNet and TrojAI (Majurski, 2020). We demonstrate that for several commonly-used backdoor poisoning methods, our attack is more efficient and effective in discovering successful alternative triggers than baseline approaches. Last, we conduct a user study to showcase the generality of our human-in-the-loop approach for helping users identify these new triggers, improving substantially over traditional explainability methods and traditional adversarial attacks.

The main contributions of this paper are as follows: (1) we consider a new thread model of poisoned classifiers where a third party aims to gain control of the poisoned classifiers without access to the original trigger, (2) we propose a interpretable, human-in-the-loop attack method under this threat model by first visualizing smoothed adversarial examples and then using human inspection to construct effective alternative triggers, (3) we demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach on constructing alternative backdoor triggers in high-resolution datasets and compare our method to existing work on modelling trigger distributions in poisoned classifiers (Qiao et al., 2019), (4) last, we conduct a user study to assess the generality of the human-in-the-loop procedure and show promising results of our approach in helping users identify poisoned classifiers.

## 2 RELATED WORK

**Backdoor Attacks** In backdoor attacks (Chen et al., 2017; Gu et al., 2017; Li et al., 2019; 2020; Weng et al., 2020; Nguyen & Tran, 2020), an adversary injects poisoned data into the training set so that at test time, clean images are misclassified into the target class when the trigger is present. BadNet (Gu et al., 2017), _Clean-label backdoor attack_ (CLBD) (Turner et al., 2019) and _Hidden trigger backdoor attack_ (HTBA) (Saha et al., 2020) achieve this by modifying a subset of training data with the backdoor trigger. Many backdoor defenses have been proposed to defend against backdoor attacks (Tran et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2019; Gao et al., 2019; Guo et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2020; Soremekun et al., 2020).

Our work is most related to defense methods based on trigger reconstruction (Wang et al., 2019; Guo et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2020; Soremekun et al., 2020). Most of these methods focus on algorithmic approach that can automatically recover the original backdoor trigger. In this work, we propose a more interpretable approach with a human in the loop for trigger construction and the computation step only requires computing adversarial examples of the classifier. Qiao et al. (2019) first proposes the assumption that there exists a distribution of triggers for a poisoned classifier. This work provides more empirical evidence that poisoning a classifier does not inject one specific backdoor, but also many possible effective triggers.

**Adversarial Robustness** Aside from backdoor attacks, another major line of work in adversarial machine learning focuses on adversarial robustness (Szegedy et al., 2013; Goodfellow et al., 2015; Madry et al., 2017; Ilyas et al., 2019), which studies the existence of imperceptibly perturbed inputs that cause misclassification in state-of-the-art classifiers. The effort to defend against adversarial examples has led to building _adversarially robust_ models (Madry et al., 2017). In addition to being

robust against adversarial examples, adversarially robust models are shown to have perceptuallyaligned gradients (Tsipras et al., 2019; Engstrom et al., 2019): adversarial examples of those classifiers show salient characteristics of other classes. This property of adversarially robust classifiers can be used, for example, to perform image synthesis (Santurkar et al., 2019).

**Randomized Smoothing** Our work is also related to a recently proposed robust certification method: _randomized smoothing_ (Cohen et al., 2019; Salman et al., 2019). Cohen et al. (2019) show that smoothing a classifier with Gaussian noise results in a _smoothed_ classifier that is certifiably robust in _l_ 2 norm. Kaur et al. (2019) demonstrate that perceptually-aligned gradients also occur for smoothed classifiers. Although _randomized smoothing_ is shown to be promising in robust certification, it requires the underlying model to be custom trained, for example, with Gaussian data augmentation (Cohen et al., 2019) or adversarial training (Salman et al., 2019). To avoid the tedious customized training, Salman et al. (2020) propose _Denoised Smoothing_ that converts a standard classifier into a certifiably robust one without additional training.

## 3 BACKGROUND

**Perceptual property of adversarially robust classifiers** Adversarially robust models are, by definition, robust to adversarial examples, where such models are usually obtained via adversarial training (Madry et al., 2017). Previous work (Tsipras et al., 2019; Santurkar et al., 2019) analyzed adversarially robust classifiers from a perceptual perspective and found that their loss gradients align well with human perception. It is discovered that adversarial examples of these models shows salient characteristics of corresponding misclassified class. Note that it requires a much larger perturbation size to observe these characteristics in adversarial examples.

**Randomized Smoothing and Denoised Smoothing** _Randomized smoothing_ (Cohen et al., 2019) is a certification procedure that converts a base classifier _f_ into a _smoothed_ classifier _g_ under Gaussian noise which is certifiably robust in _l_ 2 norm (noise level _σ_ controls the tradeoff between robustness and accuracy):

For randomized smoothing to be effective, it usually requires the base classifier _f_ to be trained via Gaussian data augmentation. _Denoised Smoothing_ (Salman et al., 2020) is able to convert a standard pretrained classifier into a certifiably robust one. It first prepends a pretrained classifier _f_ with a custom-trained denoiser _D_ , then applies randomized smoothing to the joint network _f ◦ D_ , resulting in a _robust smoothed_ classifier _f_[smoothed] :
Note that the goal of adding the denoiser is to convert the noisy input _x_ + _δ_ into clean image _x_ since the base classifier _f_ is assumed to classify _x_ well.

**Backdoor poisoning model** For backdoor attacks, we use the commonly-used ways to inject backdoor: image patching (Gu et al., 2017; Turner et al., 2019; Saha et al., 2020). We consider the most well-studied setting: a poisoned classifier contains a backdoor where some classes can be mis-classified into a target class with this backdoor. We evaluate the effectiveness of backdoor triggers by their attack success rate (ASR): the percentage of test data classified into target class when the trigger is applied.

## 4 METHODOLOGY

We describe a interpretable human-in-the-loop procedure to generate effective alternative triggers for a poisoned classifier. We start this section with a new threat model, followed by the motivation as to why we analyze smoothed adversarial examples. Then we describe our approach in detail. Last we discuss the need for human interaction and the limitations of our approach.

**Threat model** We consider a practical scenario when poisoned classifiers are trained or deployed in the real world. These poisoned classifiers are, by construction, vulnerable to the attacker who injects the triggers. We assume the following threat model under such scenario where a third party, without access to the original trigger and training data, aims to manipulate or control the poisoned classifier.

## 4.1 MOTIVATION FOR GENERATING SMOOTHED ADVERSARIAL EXAMPLES

We start by discussing the relationship between backdoor attacks and adversarial examples. Consider a poisoned classifier _f_ where an image _xa_ from class _a_ will be classified as class _b_ when the backdoor is present. Denote the application of the backdoor to image _x_ as _B_ ( _x_ ). Then for a poisoned classifier:

_f_ ( _xa_ ) = _a, f_ ( _B_ ( _xa_ )) = _b_ (3)

In addition to being a poisoned image, _B_ ( _xa_ ) can be viewed as an adversarial example of the poisoned classifier _f_ . Formally, _B_ ( _xa_ ) is an adversarial example with perturbation size _ϵ_ = _∥B_ ( _xa_ ) _− xa∥p_ in _lp_ norm:

_B_ ( _xa_ ) _∈{x | f_ ( _x_ ) _̸_ = _a, ∥x − xa∥p ≤ ϵ}_ (4)

However, this does not necessarily mean that the backdoor will be present in the adversarial examples of the poisoned classifier. This is because poisoned classifiers are themselves typically deep networks trained using traditional SGD, which are susceptible to small perturbations in the input (Szegedy et al., 2013), and loss gradients of such standard classifier are often noisy and meaningless to human perception (Tsipras et al., 2019). Thus, we propose to robustify poisoned classifiers with _Denoised Smoothing_ (Salman et al., 2020). Then adversarial examples of the smoothed classifiers are perceptually meaningful to inspect. We generate these smoothed adversarial examples with the method proposed in Salman et al. (2019). Specifically, we use the SMOOTHADVPGD method in Salman et al. (2019) and sample Monte-Carlo noise vectors to estimate the gradients of the _smoothed_ classifier. Adversarial examples are generated with a _l_ 2 norm bound _ϵ_ .

## 4.2 BREAKING POISONED CLASSIFIERS

Our overall strategy is to analyze the adversarial examples of _robustified_ poisoned classifiers. Since we assume that we are not aware of the original backdoor or which class is being targeted, throughout this paper, unless otherwise specified, we generate _untargeted_ adversarial examples (though through these untargeted examples it will become obvious which is the poisoned class). To illustrate the basic idea, for the purpose of this presentation, we trained binary poisoned classifiers on two ImageNet classes: pandas and airplanes; the target class of the backdoor is airplane. We used BadNet (Gu et al., 2017) for backdoor poisoning. After training, and without access to any training data, we then applied _Denoised Smoothing_ to create a robust version of the classifier.

In Figure 2, we show _l_ 2 adversarial panda images ( _ϵ_ = 20 _/_ 60) of the _robust_ version of two poisoned classifiers and a clean classifier[1] . Two backdoor triggers are shown in Figure 4, where Trigger A is a 30 _×_ 30 synthetic trigger with random colors, created in the

The crucial point here is that for adversarial examples of _robustified_ poisoned classifiers, there are local color regions that are immediately visually apparent. For larger perturbation size ( _ϵ_ = 60), these colors become more saturated despite background noise. While for a clean classifier, such regions are much less prevalent.

To better understand the relationship between these color regions and the backdoor, we trained poisoned classifiers with triggers from a color series, ending with a trigger of random noise. Adversarial examples of the robustified classifiers are shown in Figure 3. Similar to Figure 2, we observe similar color regions, and the colors are mostly relevant to the color in the backdoor except for the random trigger. This suggests that these local color spots can provide useful information (i.e., color) of the initial trigger.

We now describe the overall attack procedure. Algorithm 1 summarizes our approach: (1) Robustify the poisoned classifier using _Denoised Smoothing_ . (2) Generate large- _ϵ_ adversarial examples of the _robustified smoothed_ classifier. (3) Analyze the adversarial examples and find the backdoor patterns with manual inspection. (4) Use the observed backdoor patterns to construct new effective triggers.

To construct alternative triggers from backdoor patterns, we use basic image editing operations to extract new triggers from the backdoor patterns. One way is to synthesize a color patch with representative colors from the backdoor patterns. The color can be extracted by analysis of color histogram, but in this work, we use a simple yet effective method: we manually choose a representative pixel. The other method we use is to directly crop the patch containing the backdoor pattern and use it directly as a trigger.

We use the constructed triggers to attack the poisoned classifier. Surprisingly, we find that although we create these triggers from only a handful of images, they generalize well to other images in the test set, attaining high attack success rates. Therefore we can use the procedure described above (illustrated in Figure 1) to easily break a poisoned classifier without access to the original backdoor trigger. Since our attack constructs the triggers from adversarial examples, one could argue that this is caused by the transferability of adversarial patches (Brown et al., 2017), which could be a general property of all classifiers (i.e., our attack may also work for clean classifier by creating an adversarial patch). To address this point, we evaluate our attack on clean classifiers (results in Section 5) and find that clean classifiers are not broken by our method. Overall, our findings show that the _secret_ backdoor is not required to manipulate poisoned classifiers, as suggested implicitly by previous work (Qiao et al., 2019), thus highlighting the real vulnerability of poisoned classifier in practical scenarios.

## 4.3 DISCUSSION

**The need for human interaction.** It is important to emphasize that the process we describe above requires _human interaction_ as part of the approach: i.e., a human analyst needs to identify “suspicious” regions in the adversarial images and select them as potential alternative triggers. However, rather

than this being a downside of our approach, we emphasize that in fact we believe this to be a _benefit_ . There are two main reasons for this. First, as discussed briefly above, the likely practical use cases of identifying poisoned classifiers is quite different than that of identifying or avoiding traditional adversarial examples. Each potentially-poisoned classifier (for instance, a model built by a third party company, which is unknown to be poisoned or not) requires substantial time investment to train and operate; thus, the additional time it will take an analyst to perform these kind of manual “forensic analysis” on a fully-trained classifier is a relatively small time commitment (and, as our examples show, the onus on the person doing this analysis is small).

Given this factor, the second reason that the human-in-the-loop nature of our process is beneficial is that human interaction is _needed_ precisely due to the fundamental nature of adversarial examples. By definition, adversarial examples are perturbations that, to a human, will not change the “true” label of an image, but will cause an algorithm to classify it differently. If we relied on automated procedures to select the “suspicious” elements in an image, it would likely be possible to construct triggers that function as adversarial examples for these detectors, and thus evade detection. It is exactly (and, arguably _only_ ) by integrating a human in the loop, which is entirely feasible in the data-poisoning use case, that we can hope to avoid the possibility of adversarial attacks against a fully automated system.

**Limitations of our method** As we consider mainly backdoor attacks with patch-based triggers, our method is limited in that it can not be directly applied to more sophisticated backdoor attacks. In addition to patch based backdoor attacks, there are works that investigate other types of trigger: social-media filters (Sarkar et al., 2020), image warping (Nguyen & Tran, 2021), watermarks (Chen et al., 2021), image blending (Chen et al., 2017) and reflectance (Liu et al., 2020). These backdoor attack methods assume a different form of trigger. In these cases, trigger construction methods for poisoned classifiers should take into account the form of the triggers.

Since we use a human-in-the-loop procedure to extract new triggers, there is not a exact algorithmic standard of whether a patch should be used for construction. As described in section 4.2, we select the patches which have dense distinctive color regions, which are most of the cases we encountered. There are also cases where such regions are hard to determine (see Figure 8a). It could be hard to justify the attack results we report: we as the authors may be biased in constructing backdoor triggers since we have much more expertise in dealing with edge cases. As an attempt to evaluate our human-in-the-loop procedure fairly, we conduct a user study on TrojAI datasets. Participants are not familiar with backdoor attacks but have basic knowledge of machine learning (details in section 5.2).

## 5 EXPERIMENTS

In this section, we present our attack results on poisoned classifiers from two datasets: ImageNet (Russakovsky et al., 2015) and TrojAI (Majurski, 2020)[2] . For _Denoised Smoothing_ , we use the MSEtrained ImageNet denoiser adopted from Salman et al. (2020). To make backdoor presence conspicuous, we synthesize large- _ϵ_ untargeted adversarial examples ( _ϵ_ = 20 _,_ 60). The noise level we use in _smoothed_ classifiers is 1.00, as Kaur et al. (2019) shows that larger noise level leads to better visual results. We refer the reader to Appendix A for details on the experimental setup. For both datasets, we construct alternative triggers of size 30 _×_ 30, same as the size of the backdoor trigger used in ImageNet poisoned classifiers[3] . We apply alternative triggers to random locations for ImageNet (same as the initial backdoor) and a fixed place near the center for TrojAI. To evaluate the attack success rate, for ImageNet, we use 50 images for binary classifier and 200 images for multi-class classifier in the test set; for TrojAI dataset, we use the released 500 sample test images for each classifier.

## 5.1 IMAGENET

We train both binary classifiers and 5-class classifiers with three backdoor attack methods: BadNet (Gu et al., 2017), HTBA (Saha et al., 2020) and CLBD (Turner et al., 2019). We adopt Trigger A in Figure 4 as the default trigger. See Appendix E for results on ImageNet classifiers with more classes.

**Visualizing adversarial examples** We compare _Denoised Smoothing_ to two baseline approaches for generating adversarial examples: adversarial examples of: 1) the poisoned classifier (denoted as _Basic Adv_ ); 2) the _smoothed_ poisoned classifier without a denoiser (denoted as _Smoothing_ ). We generate adversarial examples ( _ϵ_ = 20) of the _robustified_ binary poisoned classifier on ImageNet, shown in Figure 5 (More examples are shown in Figure 18 in Appendix D.). First, we can see that our approach gives less noisy and smoother adversarial images than baselines. Second, there are some vague backdoor patterns in _Basic Adv_ , but backdoor patterns in adversarial examples from _Denoised Smoothing_ are more distinctive and easier to recognize. Last, _Smoothing_ baseline does not produce any obvious pattern, which highlights the necessity of _Denoised Smoothing_ .

**Attack results on poisoned classifiers** In Figure 6, we present sample alternative backdoor triggers we constructed by attacking a BadNet poisoned multi-class classifier on ImageNet, where we show both color patch and cropped patch constructed from each adversarial example. For attack results on other five ImageNet poisoned classifiers, we refer the reader to Figure 11 and Figure 12 in Appendix C. From Figure 6, we can see that all the alternative triggers created from backdoor patterns have relatively high success rate. In particular, two triggers achieve significantly higher attack success rate (89 _._ 20% _,_ 85 _._ 80%) than the original backdoor Trigger A (72 _._ 60%). Also notice that these alternative triggers differ greatly from Trigger A. Last, we can see that whether color patch or cropped patch perform better depends on each example. In terms of the effect of perturbation size, it can be seen that larger epsilon leads to better attack results.

We compare our method with two baselines: (1) a trigger distribution modeling method (Qiao et al., 2019), (2) randomly cropped patches from smoothed adversarial examples. Although Qiao et al. (2019) initially proposes MESA to model the distribution of triggers as a part of their defense method, here we use MESA to sample from this trigger distribution and compare with the alternative triggers discovered by our method. A summary of attack results is shown in Table 1, where we report the ASR of: triggers constructed by our method, triggers constructed by MESA (Qiao et al., 2019) and the original backdoor. It can be seen that our attack finds much more effective triggers than the MESA baseline, and also the original trigger. For example, for the binary BadNet classifier, our method construct triggers with ASR 98 _._ 80% while the triggers constructed by MESA attains only

**Attack results on clean classifiers** We show that clean classifiers are not broken under our attack. Note that clean classifiers are not poisoned and there is no such concept as attack success rate for clean classifiers. To measure the effect of the triggers constructed by our procedure on clean classifiers, we report the error rate of clean classifiers when the test data is patched by the alternative triggers. Figure 7 presents an illustration for attacking a clean multi-class ImageNet classifier with our approach. We refer the reader to Figure 13 in Appendix C for more results on attacking clean classifiers. Here we choose larger perturbation size _ϵ_ = 60 because we find no obvious pattern with perturbation size _ϵ_ = 20. Observe that clean classifiers have low error rates with test data patched by the constructed triggers, meaning that our attack does not apply to clean classifiers.

**“Camouflaged” Backdoor** So far we have experimented with triggers that contain colors (i.e., red, blue in Trigger A) that are visually distinctive and as a result, backdoor patterns can be easily recognizable in adversarial examples. We study the case when backdoor trigger is less colorful or contains colors already in the color distribution of clean images. Consider Trigger C in Figure 8a: black and white colors in this trigger are also representative colors of a panda. We train a poisoned binary classifier on ImageNet using Trigger C as the backdoor, where the backdoor attack method is BadNet (Gu et al., 2017). In Figure 8a, we visualize adversarial examples of the _robustified_ poisoned classifier. Although there is no clear backdoor pattern in the form of dense color regions, we can observe that in the generated adversarial examples, there is a tendency for black regions to have vertical or horizontal boundaries, which resembles the pattern in Trigger C. Despite the absence of obvious backdoor patterns, we are still able to break the poisoned classifier using cropped patterns from large- _ϵ_ ( _ϵ_ = 100) adversarial examples as shown in Figure 8b. Notice that both of the triggers are noisy and seem completely different from Trigger C, but they attain higher attack success rate (88 _._ 60% and 83 _._ 00%) than the original backdoor (75 _._ 80%).

## 5.2 TROJAI

A dataset in TrojAI (Majurski, 2020) consists of a mixed set of clean and poisoned classifiers. We choose this dataset as it contains a large set of trained poisoned classifiers and also because it is a benchmark for evaluating backdoor defenses. Different from ImageNet, we are not aware of the exact backdoor triggers used to poison the classifiers. TrojAI contains four image datasets: from round 0

**Attack results** In Figure 9, we show attack results on a poisoned classifier sampled from TrojAI dataset. As shown in Figure 9, our methods can attack these poisoned classifiers with high success rate (See Figure 15 in Appendix C for results on more poisoned classifiers.). Similarly, the cropped trigger achieves higher success rate than the color trigger for both classifiers. For complete evaluation, we randomly sample 20 classifiers each from round 0 to 3 datasets (excluding filter based triggers) and apply our attack. The average attack success rate (ASR) is summarized in Table 2. We can see that the constructed triggers by our method have high ASR on poisoned classifiers.

**Human study** We conduct a user study on the TrojAI dataset (for simplicity, we choose TrojAI round 0 data). Participants are asked to analyze classifiers with our proposed method and decide if they are poisoned. We develop an interactive tool implementing our method to aid the study. Two control groups are used: 1) a variant of our method which uses adversarial examples of the original classifier (denoted as _Basic Adv_ ); 2) saliency maps on clean images (denoted as _Saliency Map_ ). In total, 15 participants from CS background with basic knowledge of machine learning take part in the human study and they are evenly divided into three groups. ## 6 CONCLUSION

In this work we introduce a new threat model of poisoned classifier where one would want to break it without access to the original trigger. We propose a human-in-the-loop approach to attack poisoned classifiers in this threat model. We observe smoothed adversarial examples of a _robustified_ poisoned classifier can contain backdoor patterns. Our attack procedure then constructs new alternative triggers with these backdoor patterns and we find that they give comparable or even better attack performance than the initial backdoor. We demonstrate that our attack is effective on high-resolution datasets, with a comparison to previous work on modelling trigger distribution. We end with a user study showcasing the efficiency and interpretability of our approach to the wider audience. Our work highlight the vulnerability of poisoned classifier to common users without access to the original trigger. From the promising results of our user study, we believe that future work for analyzing model robustness or image classifiers can benefit from a human-in-the-loop approach.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: The paper is clearly written, well-motivated, and addresses a compelling paradigm shift in backdoor research: the notion that poisoning creates a structural vulnerability rather than just a private exploit. The methodology—leveraging "Denoised Smoothing" to generate perceptually-aligned gradients for trigger discovery—is intellectually sound and effectively bridges the gap between robust statistics and model security. However, the most significant issue lies in the reliance on "human-in-the-loop" interaction as a core component of the attack’s performance. While this is an interesting diagnostic tool, its subjectivity threatens the rigor of the "attack success rate" metrics; it is unclear how the authors control for human bias or how reproducible these "extracted triggers" would be if performed by a different user. To strengthen the paper, the authors should be careful to explicitly decouple the *automated discovery* of visual features from the *manual construction* of triggers, perhaps by quantifying the extent to which human intervention is necessary versus what could be automated post-visualization.

progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: This paper presents a compelling and timely security insight: that poisoned classifiers are not exclusively controlled by the original adversary, as third parties can generate alternative backdoor triggers without access to the original trigger or training data. The proposed methodology—leveraging denoised smoothing to obtain perceptually-aligned adversarial examples and extracting reusable trigger patches via a human-in-the-loop pipeline—is a creative application of certified robustness techniques to the backdoor threat model. However, the manuscript currently exhibits notable clarity and presentation shortcomings, including frequent grammatical errors and typos (e.g., “a attack procedure,” “new thread model,” “a interpretable,” “requi”), which detract from its academic rigor. More substantively, the conceptual bridge between smoothed adversarial examples and exploitable backdoor structures remains under-motivated; the authors must provide a clearer theoretical or empirical justification for why adversarial sensitivity in a denoised-smoothed model reliably surfaces generalizable trigger patterns, and how this approach conceptually and practically diverges from established trigger reconstruction/inversion baselines. Additionally, the abstract and introduction occasionally rely on sensationalized framing (“fundamentally broken,” “no such thing as a secret backdoor”) that would benefit from more measured, evidence-driven language. Addressing these clarity issues, strengthening the methodological rationale, and precisely positioning the work against contemporary trigger-recovery literature will significantly elevate the paper’s quality and impact.

Second, it introduces an $\ell_p$ norm bound without justification, contradicting the methodology's explicit use of an $\ell_2$ bound $\epsilon$. Third, the loss function $L(\cdot, y)$ is undefined. Finally, adversarial attack generation is formulated as $\arg\min L$, which is technically reversed for standalone attacks (should be $\arg\max L$ or $\arg\min -L$). comments.explanation: The isolated '65 _._ 29%.' appears abruptly between the Figure 8 caption and the subsequent paragraph. comments.title: Threat model assumptions vs. real-world API constraints
comments.quote: This third party is allowed to perform whatever analysis necessary on the poisoned classifiers with test data. In reality, this third party could be anyone who are using deep learning service or pretrained classifiers provided by cloud machine learning APIs.
comments.explanation: The threat model assumes an adversary can perform 'whatever analysis necessary,' including generating large volumes of adversarial examples. However, the authors claim this applies to general cloud ML APIs. In practice, commercial APIs enforce strict query rate limits, request caps, and often restrict batch processing or probing. The threat model glosses over these practical constraints, slightly overstating the feasibility of the attack against deployed services without acknowledging rate-limiting or authentication barriers.

comments.title: Author name ordering error in Badnets reference
comments.quote: Tianyu Gu, Dolan-Gavitt Brendan, and Siddharth Garg. Badnets: Identifying vulnerabilities in the machine learning model supply chain. _arXiv preprint arXiv:1708.06733_ , 2017.
comments.explanation: The bibliography entry lists the second author as 'Dolan-Gavitt Brendan' instead of the conventional 'Brendan Dolan-Gavitt'. Standard academic conventions and the original arXiv submission confirm the correct First Name + Last Name order. This is a minor OCR/typesetting artifact that swapped given and family names. It does not impact technical validity but stands out as a bibliographic inaccuracy.

progressive_original__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive Original (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive_original__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: This paper presents a compelling and timely security insight: that poisoned classifiers are not exclusively controlled by the original adversary, as third parties can generate alternative backdoor triggers without access to the original trigger or training data. The proposed methodology—leveraging denoised smoothing to obtain perceptually-aligned adversarial examples and extracting reusable trigger patches via a human-in-the-loop pipeline—is a creative application of certified robustness techniques to the backdoor threat model. However, the manuscript currently exhibits notable clarity and presentation shortcomings, including frequent grammatical errors and typos (e.g., “a attack procedure,” “new thread model,” “a interpretable,” “requi”), which detract from its academic rigor. More substantively, the conceptual bridge between smoothed adversarial examples and exploitable backdoor structures remains under-motivated; the authors must provide a clearer theoretical or empirical justification for why adversarial sensitivity in a denoised-smoothed model reliably surfaces generalizable trigger patterns, and how this approach conceptually and practically diverges from established trigger reconstruction/inversion baselines. Additionally, the abstract and introduction occasionally rely on sensationalized framing (“fundamentally broken,” “no such thing as a secret backdoor”) that would benefit from more measured, evidence-driven language. Addressing these clarity issues, strengthening the methodological rationale, and precisely positioning the work against contemporary trigger-recovery literature will significantly elevate the paper’s quality and impact.

comments.title: Typographical error: "thread model" instead of "threat model"
comments.quote: (1) we consider a new thread model of poisoned classifiers where a third party aims to gain control of the poisoned classifiers without access to the original trigger,
comments.explanation: The authors clearly intend to refer to a 'threat model', which is a standard term in security literature and is used consistently elsewhere in the paper (e.g., in the Abstract and earlier in the Introduction). 'Thread' appears to be a simple typo. It does not undermine the technical content or methodology but momentarily disrupts reading flow.

comments.title: Ambiguous pronoun reference in threat model setup
comments.quote: We describe a new threat model for poisoned classifier, where one without knowledge of the original trigger, would want to control the poisoned classifier.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'where one without knowledge...' is grammatically awkward and initially vague. 'One' likely refers to a hypothetical third-party attacker or analyst, but the specific actor is not named until later in the introduction ('a third party'). While context quickly resolves the intent, the phrasing here could momentarily mislead a careful reader regarding who is attempting to control the classifier.

comments.title: Minor grammatical agreement and article errors
comments.quote: find that our method are more scalable and efficient in generating effective triggers.
comments.explanation: The text contains minor grammatical slips, such as 'our method are' (should be 'is') and missing articles ('a attack', 'a interpretable'). These are draft-quality prose issues that do not affect the technical claims, mathematical notation, or experimental setup. The claim about scalability/efficiency is a forward reference to results discussed in later sections, which is standard and acceptable in an abstract/introduction.

comments.title: Missing mathematical definition for smoothed classifier
comments.quote:
converts a base classifier _f_ into a _smoothed_ classifier _g_ under Gaussian noise which is certifiably robust in _l_ 2 norm (noise level _σ_ controls the tradeoff between robustness and accuracy):

For randomized smoothing to be effective...
comments.explanation: Initially, I noticed a colon immediately followed by a paragraph break where a mathematical definition or equation should appear. Reading the surrounding text, it is clear the authors intend to introduce the formal definition of the smoothed classifier $g(x)$ and its certification guarantee. In standard randomized smoothing literature (Cohen et al., 2019), this is given by $g(x) = \text{argmax}_c P(f(x+\epsilon)=c)$ where $\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2 I)$, along with a theorem stating certifiable $\ell_2$ robustness radius. While this is almost certainly a PDF extraction/OCR artifact dropping display math, it leaves the theoretical foundation of Section 3 incomplete. A careful reader cannot verify the $\ell_2$ claim or understand how $\sigma$ parameterizes the classifier without seeing the standard formulation.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency between composition operator and procedure name
comments.quote: Section 3: "...joint network _f ◦ D_ , resulting in a _robust smoothed_ classifier _f_[smoothed] :" vs Algorithm 1: "1: Convert the poisoned classifier _f_ into a smoothed robust one _DS · f_ ."
comments.explanation: In Section 3, the authors define $D$ as a custom-trained denoiser prepended to classifier $f$, writing the joint forward pass as $f \circ D$ (implying $f(D(x))$). However, in Algorithm 1, the same transformation is written as `DS · f`. If `DS` refers to the Denoised Smoothing procedure introduced in the algorithm input, using a composition operator `·` between a procedure acronym and a classifier is ambiguous. It is unclear whether `DS` denotes a function, an operator, or simply a shorthand for $D$. This inconsistency between a formal composition notation and a procedural application notation could confuse readers attempting to implement or formally analyze the pipeline.

comments.title: Underspecified perturbation variable and norm bound in Algorithm 1
comments.quote: "2: Compute smoothed adversarial examples: _xadv_ = arg min _∥x∗−x∥p L_ ( _DS · f_ ( _x[∗]_ ) _, y_ )."
comments.explanation: I initially flagged the alternating notation `x*` and `x[*]` as likely OCR artifacts, which they probably are. However, even assuming the most plausible intended symbol ($x^*$), the equation introduces an $\ell_p$ norm bound without justification, whereas the preceding methodology section explicitly states: "Adversarial examples are generated with a _l_ 2 norm bound _ϵ_ ." Switching to a general $\ell_p$ norm in the algorithm step without explaining why or how $p$ is chosen (or if it should be $p=2$) is an underspecification that could mislead a reproducer. Additionally, the loss function $L(\cdot, y)$ is never defined or referenced in this section, leaving the exact optimization objective ambiguous.

comments.title: Inconsistent composition notation for Denoised Smoothing
comments.quote: f ◦ D (Section 3) vs DS · f (Algorithm 1, Step 1)
comments.explanation: In Section 3, the authors formally define the Denoised Smoothing pipeline as prepending a denoiser D to a base classifier f, forming the joint network 'f ◦ D' (where standard mathematical notation implies D is applied first, then f). However, in Algorithm 1, Step 1, they write 'DS · f'. While 'DS' is clearly intended as a procedural operator applied to f, the use of the dot/asterisk notation deviates from the established 'f ◦ D' symbol and introduces slight ambiguity (it could be misread as matrix multiplication or a different compositional order). For notation consistency and reproducibility, the authors should either reuse 'f ◦ D' in the algorithm or explicitly define 'DS · f' as a shorthand for the smoothing procedure applied to f.

comments.title: Ambiguous perturbation size notation
comments.explanation: The parenthetical '( _ϵ_ = 20 _/_ 60)' appears to be a typographical error or PDF-to-OCR artifact where a comma was replaced by a forward slash. As written, '20 / 60' could be misinterpreted as a ratio (0.333) rather than two distinct l2-norm perturbation bounds. Given the surrounding context—specifically, the later mention of 'For larger perturbation size ( _ϵ_ = 60 )'—the authors clearly intend to report two separate experimental settings. Replacing the slash with a comma '( _ϵ_ = 20, 60 )' would align with standard scientific reporting and prevent misinterpretation.

comments.title: Underspecified and inconsistent optimization variable in Algorithm 1
comments.quote: xadv = arg min _∥x∗−x∥p L_ ( _DS · f_ ( _x[∗]_ ) _, y_ ).
comments.explanation: Algorithm 1, Step 2, uses 'x∗' as the minimization variable inside the norm constraint but switches to 'x[∗]' as the input to the classifier. This inconsistency is non-standard and obscures the intended mathematical formulation. It is highly likely an OCR/formatting artifact for a primed variable (e.g., 'x' or 'x''), but as presented, it leaves ambiguous whether a specific iterative notation was intended or if a simple surrogate variable was meant. In PGD-style optimizations, a clear, distinct variable (like 'z' or 'x'') is standard to avoid confusion with the original input 'x'. Clarifying this notation would improve the algorithm's technical precision and reproducibility.

comments.title: Ambiguous terminology regarding 'noise level' in smoothed classifiers
comments.quote: The noise level we use in _smoothed_ classifiers is 1.00, as Kaur et al. (2019) shows that larger noise level leads to better visual results.
comments.explanation: The authors state they use a noise level of 1.00 'in smoothed classifiers' and justify it by citing Kaur et al. (2019) on visual results. In the randomized smoothing literature, the 'noise level' typically refers to the standard deviation parameter $\sigma$ used during the smoothing certification process. A larger $\sigma$ generally decreases base classification accuracy while increasing the certified robustness radius, but it does not inherently yield 'better visual results' for adversarial examples. The cited Kaur et al. (2019) paper actually focuses on generating *smoothed adversarial examples*, where a noise scale is applied during gradient estimation (e.g., via Monte Carlo sampling) to stabilize perturbations and improve interpretability. The current phrasing conflates the smoothing certification parameter $\sigma$ with the noise scale used for smoothadv optimization. Given that the authors explicitly mention using Monte Carlo noise vectors for gradient estimation in Section 4.1, the 1.00 value likely refers to that optimization noise scale rather than the smoothing parameter. Clarifying which parameter is set to 1.00 and its precise role would prevent misinterpretation during reproduction. The methodology is sound, but the parameter description contains a minor technical ambiguity.

comments.title: Dangling numerical value disrupting paragraph flow
comments.quote: 65 _._ 29%. Compared to randomly cropped patches, we can see that human interaction is helpful to finding effective triggers in our approach.
comments.explanation: The isolated '65 _._ 29%.' appears abruptly between the Figure 8 caption and the subsequent paragraph. Given the surrounding OCR layout artifacts (e.g., table captions and table cells bleeding into the main text), this is almost certainly a stray numerical value from a table or figure annotation that failed to render in its proper location. While it does not alter the core technical argument, it creates unnecessary ambiguity about what metric it represents and breaks the logical flow. A careful reader would have to infer it is an extraction error rather than a deliberate data point tied to the discussion of Trigger C.

comments.title: Optimization direction for adversarial attack generation
comments.quote: Compute smoothed adversarial examples: _xadv_ = arg min _∥x∗−x∥p L_ ( _DS · f_ ( _x[∗]_ ) _, y_ ).
comments.explanation: The authors formulate adversarial example generation as an `arg min` of the loss function `L`. Standard adversarial attacks seek to maximize `L` (or equivalently, minimize `-L`) to find inputs that cause misclassification. The `arg min L` formulation is typically reserved for adversarial training objectives (optimizing both model parameters and perturbations). While the paper consistently uses this notation throughout the methodology and background sections, it is technically reversed for a standalone attack generation step. Clarifying that the optimization minimizes the negative loss, or explicitly stating `arg max L`, would align the notation with standard adversarial literature conventions and prevent confusion for reproducibility.

comments.title: Threat model assumptions vs. real-world API constraints
comments.quote: This third party is allowed to perform whatever analysis necessary on the poisoned classifiers with test data. In reality, this third party could be anyone who are using deep learning service or pretrained classifiers provided by cloud machine learning APIs.
comments.explanation: The authors posit a threat model where an adversary has query access to the classifier and can perform 'whatever analysis necessary,' including generating large-ϵ adversarial examples across many test samples for both automated optimization and human-in-the-loop inspection. However, they also claim this applies to general cloud ML APIs. In practice, cloud APIs enforce strict query rate limits, request caps, and often restrict batch processing or probing for adversarial inputs. Generating the volume of adversarial examples required for the proposed pipeline would likely be throttled or blocked in real API deployments. The threat model glosses over these practical query constraints, which slightly overstates the feasibility of the attack against commercially deployed services without acknowledging rate-limiting or authentication barriers.

progressive__glm-4.7-flash.label: Progressive (glm-4.7-flash)
progressive__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This paper makes a compelling and significant argument that poisoned classifiers are "fundamentally broken" and accessible to third parties, rather than being secure backdoors controlled exclusively by the original attacker. The proposed methodology—utilizing Denoised Smoothing to convert the poisoned model into a smooth, robust classifier and extracting perceptual gradients via a human-in-the-loop approach—represents a novel and interpretable attack vector for reverse-engineering triggers. While the writing is logically structured and the core contribution is technically sound, the manuscript currently suffers from distracting formatting errors, specifically the inclusion of a raw figure caption in the middle of the text between Figure 1 and Section 2. This artifact disrupts the reading flow and needs to be removed or formatted as a standard caption. Furthermore, while the distinction between the new threat model and the traditional one is clear in the abstract, a more explicit contrast in the introduction would further solidify the novelty of the work.

comments.title: Ambiguous perturbation size notation in Figure 2
comments.quote: Visualization of some adversarial examples ( ϵ = 20 _/_ 60) from two _robustified_ poisoned classifiers and a _robustified_ clean classifier.
comments.explanation: The notation 'ϵ = 20 / 60' is ambiguous; it could represent a range (20 to 60), a list (20, 60), or a single value (20/60 ≈ 0.33). This is inconsistent with Figure 3 and Figure 5, which explicitly state 'ϵ = 20'. While it is possible the authors use different perturbation sizes for different visualizations, the slash notation is non-standard and confuses the specific parameter used. The context suggests this might be an OCR artifact for a comma or range, but it remains a real ambiguity in the provided text.

comments.title: Ambiguous algorithm notation
comments.quote: Convert the poisoned classifier f into a smoothed robust one DS · f.
comments.explanation: The use of the dot symbol (·) in pseudo-code to denote the output of a procedure (Denoised Smoothing, denoted DS) applied to an input (f) is unconventional and potentially confusing. The dot is typically used for multiplication or element-wise operations. A standard function application notation such as DS(f) or a distinct variable name for the smoothed classifier would be clearer and consistent with the notation f ◦ D used earlier in the text to denote composition.

comments.title: Grammatical error in Abstract
comments.quote: ...find that our method are more scalable and efficient in generating effective triggers.
comments.explanation: The subject 'our method' is singular, so the verb 'are' should be 'is'.

comments.title: Grammatical error in Introduction
comments.quote: Then we propose a attack procedure showing that...
comments.explanation: The article 'a' is incorrect before a word starting with a vowel sound. It should be 'an attack'.

comments.title: Vague terminology in Trigger A description
comments.quote: Trigger A is a 30 × 30 synthetic trigger with random colors, created in the backdoor attack method HTBA (Saha et al., 2020)
comments.explanation: HTBA (Hidden Trigger Backdoor Attack) typically refers to injecting hidden triggers (often noise). Describing a trigger as a 'synthetic trigger with random colors' contradicts the notion of a 'hidden' trigger, which is imperceptible. While the follow-up text clarifies this by mentioning 'random noise', the initial description creates confusion about whether the trigger is a visible patch or a noise pattern.

comments.title: Citation Year Mismatch for SMOOTHADVPGD
comments.quote: Specifically, we use the SMOOTHADVPGD method in Salman et al. (2019) and sample Monte-Carlo noise vectors to estimate the gradients of the smoothed classifier.
comments.explanation: The term 'SMOOTHADVPGD' is a specific algorithm name associated with the paper 'Smooth Adversarial Training' (Salman et al., 2020). The paper 'Simple Black-box Adversarial Examples for Convolutional Networks' (Salman et al., 2019) introduced SimpleBB, an untargeted method. While the procedure of generating smoothed adversarial examples via noise sampling can apply to untargeted attacks, citing 'SMOOTHADVPGD' with the year 2019 is factually incorrect and likely a typo in the citation year.

comments.title: OCR/Notation Artifact in Equation
comments.quote: resulting in a _robust smoothed_ classifier _f_[smoothed] :
comments.explanation: The notation `f_[smoothed]` appears inconsistent and broken. In LaTeX, subscripts are denoted by curly braces (e.g., `f_{smoothed}`), and brackets are usually not used for this purpose without specific operators. This is clearly an OCR artifact from a formatting error (likely from `f_{smooth}` or `f^*`). While the context clearly indicates this represents the smoothed version of the classifier, the visual representation is inconsistent with mathematical notation and may confuse the reader.

comments.title: Missing constraint in adversarial example minimization
comments.quote: x_{adv} = \arg \min_{||x^* - x||_p} L(DS \cdot f(x[*]), y)
comments.explanation: The equation defines the generation of adversarial examples but the minimization operator is subscripted incorrectly. The constraint for adversarial perturbation should be applied to the input perturbation (e.g., \(\min_{||x^* - x||_p \le \epsilon}\)). Subscripting \(||x^* - x||_p\) alone implies minimizing the norm itself, which would simply return the original input \(x\). While \(\epsilon\) is mentioned in the surrounding text (Section 5.1) as being 20 or 60, it is absent from this defining equation, making the optimization problem ill-defined as written.

comments.title: TrojAI dataset sample count
comments.quote: For TrojAI dataset, we use the released 500 sample test images for each classifier.
comments.explanation: The paper states that 500 test images were used for each classifier. While this is presented as a specific numerical value, standard TrojAI benchmarks (TrojAI-5k, TrojAI-10k, etc.) typically use thousands of samples. Without explicit clarification that these are a subset (e.g., "a subset of 500"), the reader might assume this is the full benchmark release. Given the small sample size compared to ImageNet (50/200/500), this constitutes an underspecified parameter that affects the statistical robustness of the reported results.

progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
## High-Level Feedback

This paper presents a compelling and provocative central claim—that backdoored classifiers are not exclusively controllable by the original adversary—and introduces a novel, human-in-the-loop attack methodology that is both creative and practically relevant. The clarity of the exposition is generally strong, with a well-structured abstract and introduction that effectively motivate the problem and situate the work within the existing literature. However, the paper suffers from several significant issues that undermine its rigor and completeness. First, the threat model, while novel, is insufficiently formalized: the authors do not clearly specify the attacker's knowledge (e.g., is the classifier API-only or white-box? Is the training dataset truly inaccessible?) or the constraints under which alternative triggers are considered "effective" (e.g., are they required to be imperceptible like the original trigger, or can they be large colored patches?). Second, the paper lacks a rigorous theoretical or empirical analysis of why Denoised Smoothing yields perceptually-aligned gradients specifically for poisoned classifiers, and whether this property holds across different architectures, poisoning ratios, and trigger types. Third, the evaluation is limited in scope: the comparison to prior work on trigger distribution modeling (Qiao et al., 2019) is superficial, and the user study, while a nice addition, is not described in sufficient detail to assess its validity or generalizability. Finally, the paper would benefit from a clearer articulation of the practical implications and limitations of the proposed attack, particularly regarding the reliance on human inspection, which raises questions about scalability and reproducibility. These issues, if addressed, would substantially strengthen the paper's contribution and impact.

comments.title: Inconsistency in adversarial example generation direction
comments.quote: xadv = arg min _∥x∗−x∥p L_ ( _DS · f_ ( _x[∗]_ ) _, y_ ).
comments.explanation: The notation in Algorithm 1 shows adversarial examples are generated by minimizing the loss (arg min), which would move the input toward the true label y. In standard adversarial attacks, examples are generated by maximizing the loss to cause misclassification. The authors refer to SMOOTHADVPGD from Salman et al. (2019), which uses gradient ascent. Given that the algorithm's purpose is to find inputs that fool the classifier, the arg min is almost certainly a typo or formatting error; it should likely be arg max. However, without correction, the text is mathematically inconsistent with the intended behavior described in the prose and with standard definitions in Section 3.

comments.title: Ambiguous notation for norm and epsilon constraint in adversarial example generation
comments.quote: xadv = arg min _∥x∗−x∥p L_ ( _DS · f_ ( _x[∗]_ ) _, y_ ).
comments.explanation: The optimization problem lacks a constraint on the perturbation size (epsilon). Standard adversarial example generation involves either an explicit constraint (e.g., ∥x∗−x∥_p ≤ ϵ) or a Lagrangian formulation. The text earlier mentions using a ℓ₂ norm bound ϵ, but Algorithm 1 omits this constraint. This could mislead a reader attempting to reproduce the method, as it suggests unconstrained minimization, which would not produce meaningful adversarial examples. Additionally, the paper does not specify the pixel value range (e.g., [0,1] or [0,255]) for the epsilon values used across experiments, causing ambiguity in reproducibility.

comments.title: Overclaim about 'obvious' poisoned class from untargeted examples
comments.quote: though through these untargeted examples it will become obvious which is the poisoned class
comments.explanation: The authors claim that from untargeted adversarial examples of the robustified classifier, it will 'become obvious' which class is the poisoned target. While the paper shows that backdoor patterns appear in adversarial images, the text does not explain how a third party, without knowledge of the original trigger or training data, would deduce that these patterns correspond to a specific target class rather than just being artifacts of the robustification process. The claim is not supported by the subsequent analysis in this section, which focuses on color similarity to the trigger, not identifying the target class. This could overstate what a user would perceive without additional analysis.

comments.title: Underspecified method for trigger construction from backdoor patterns
comments.quote: we use a simple yet effective method: we manually choose a representative pixel. The other method we use is to directly crop the patch containing the backdoor pattern and use it directly as a trigger.
comments.explanation: The description of 'manually choosing a representative pixel' to synthesize a color patch is underspecified. It is unclear how a single pixel defines a color patch—what size should the patch be? Should it be solid or have spatial structure? Similarly, 'cropping the patch' is vague: how is the patch boundary determined? These details are crucial for reproducibility, especially because the attack success rate depends on the trigger's shape and placement. While the authors note human interaction is involved, the algorithmic steps for constructing the final trigger from the identified region are not specified, leaving room for ambiguity. The criteria for what constitutes a 'suspicious' region is also not formally defined.

comments.title: Overstatement about clean classifiers not being broken by the method
comments.quote: To address this point, we evaluate our attack on clean classifiers (results in Section 5) and find that clean classifiers are not broken by our method.
comments.explanation: The authors claim clean classifiers are 'not broken by our method' based on experiments in Section 5. The actual result in Figure 7 shows error rates of 1.24% to 8.04% for color/cropped patches on clean classifiers. While these are low, they are not zero, meaning the method does produce some misclassifications on clean classifiers. The wording 'not broken' could be interpreted as 'zero attack success rate', which is unlikely. Additionally, the claim that the vulnerability is specific to poisoned classifiers is weakened because adversarial patches are known to transfer across models, including clean classifiers. A statistical test comparing ASR on poisoned vs. clean classifiers would be more rigorous.

## High-Level Feedback

This paper presents a compelling and provocative central claim—that backdoored classifiers are not exclusively controllable by the original adversary—and introduces a novel, human-in-the-loop attack methodology that is both creative and practically relevant. The clarity of the exposition is generally strong, with a well-structured abstract and introduction that effectively motivate the problem and situate the work within the existing literature. However, the paper suffers from several significant issues that undermine its rigor and completeness. First, the threat model, while novel, is insufficiently formalized: the authors do not clearly specify the attacker's knowledge (e.g., is the classifier API-only or white-box? Is the training dataset truly inaccessible?) or the constraints under which alternative triggers are considered "effective" (e.g., are they required to be imperceptible like the original trigger, or can they be large colored patches?). Second, the paper lacks a rigorous theoretical or empirical analysis of why Denoised Smoothing yields perceptually-aligned gradients specifically for poisoned classifiers, and whether this property holds across different architectures, poisoning ratios, and trigger types. Third, the evaluation is limited in scope: the comparison to prior work on trigger distribution modeling (Qiao et al., 2019) is superficial, and the user study, while a nice addition, is not described in sufficient detail to assess its validity or generalizability. Finally, the paper would benefit from a clearer articulation of the practical implications and limitations of the proposed attack, particularly regarding the reliance on human inspection, which raises questions about scalability and reproducibility. These issues, if addressed, would substantially strengthen the paper's contribution and impact.

comments.title: Ambiguous claim about 'high attack success rates' without precise quantification in methodology section
comments.quote: Surprisingly, we find that although we create these triggers from only a handful of images, they generalize well to other images in the test set, attaining high attack success rates.
comments.explanation: The authors state that the constructed triggers achieve 'high attack success rates' in Section 4.2. While this phrasing is informal and acceptable in an overview, the more precise quantification appears later in Section 5.1 (Table 1). Given the paper context and that this is part of the methodology description, the claim is sufficiently supported by later results. No issue remains.

comments.title: Overstatement about clean classifiers not being broken by the method
comments.quote: To address this point, we evaluate our attack on clean classifiers (results in Section 5) and find that clean classifiers are not broken by our method.
comments.explanation: The authors claim clean classifiers are 'not broken by our method' based on experiments in Section 5. However, the wording 'not broken' could be interpreted as 'zero attack success rate', which is unlikely. The actual result in Figure 7 shows error rates of 1.24% to 8.04% for color/cropped patches on clean classifiers. While these are low, they are not zero, meaning the method does produce some misclassifications on clean classifiers. However, the context clarifies that the claim is comparative—clean classifiers are far less susceptible than poisoned ones. The phrasing is slightly hyperbolic but not misleading given the numerical results provided. No major issue.

comments.title: Potential inconsistency in epsilon values for adversarial examples
comments.quote: To make backdoor presence conspicuous, we synthesize large-ϵ untargeted adversarial examples (ϵ = 20, 60).
comments.explanation: The paper earlier (Section 4.1) defines epsilon as the ℓ_p norm perturbation bound. In Section 5, epsilon values of 20 and 60 are used. For ImageNet images (typically 224×224×3, pixel values in [0,255]), an ℓ_2 perturbation of 20 corresponds to a per-pixel change of about 0.089 on average across all pixels (assuming uniform distribution), which is quite subtle. An epsilon of 60 is more noticeable. The use of large epsilon is justified by the need for perceptually visible patterns. However, the paper does not specify the pixel value range (e.g., [0,1] or [0,255]) for these epsilon values. In Denoised Smoothing (Salman et al., 2020), inputs are typically normalized to [0,1]; if so, ϵ=60 would be enormous. If the authors use [0,255] scaling, then ϵ=60 is reasonable. This ambiguity could confuse readers trying to reproduce results. A brief clarification of the pixel value range would resolve this.

comments.title: Underspecified method for color extraction from backdoor patterns
comments.quote: One way is to synthesize a color patch with representative colors from the backdoor patterns. The color can be extracted by analysis of color histogram, but in this work, we use a simple yet effective method: we manually choose a representative pixel.
comments.explanation: The method for constructing color patches is described only as 'manually choose a representative pixel'. This is very vague—how is the pixel selected? Is it the most saturated pixel? A pixel in the center of the colored region? The lack of specification means the procedure is not reproducible. While the paper acknowledges this as a human-in-the-loop approach, the exact human decision rule is not defined. This could affect the attack success rate results, as different human choices might yield different triggers. Given that reproducibility is a key concern, this is a moderate gap.

comments.title: Unclear transferability claim vs. property of poisoned classifiers
comments.quote: Since our attack constructs the triggers from adversarial examples, one could argue that this is caused by the transferability of adversarial patches (Brown et al., 2017), which could be a general property of all classifiers (i.e., our attack may also work for clean classifier by creating an adversarial patch). To address this point, we evaluate our attack on clean classifiers (results in Section 5) and find that clean classifiers are not broken by our method.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that because clean classifiers are not broken, the vulnerability is specific to poisoned classifiers. However, adversarial patches (Brown et al., 2017) are known to transfer across models, including clean classifiers. The authors' counterargument would be stronger if they showed that the alternative triggers do not function as universal adversarial patches on clean classifiers. The results in Figure 7 show error rates of 1.24%–8.04% on clean classifiers, which is low but non-zero. The claim 'not broken' is qualitative; a statistical test comparing ASR on poisoned vs. clean classifiers would be more rigorous. Still, the context partially addresses the concern, so this is a minor overclaim.

comments.quote: resulting in a _robust smoothed_ classifier _f_[smoothed] :
comments.explanation: The text introduces 'smoothed classifier g' and 'robust smoothed classifier f[smoothed]' but the exact definition of f[smoothed] is not given in the provided context. The notation appears in Section 3, where it says 'resulting in a _robust smoothed_ classifier _f_[smoothed] :' but the equation following this is missing (likely truncated by OCR). Given the surrounding context, it's clear they intend the composition of the denoiser D and classifier f, with randomized smoothing applied. However, without the explicit equation, a reader cannot verify the precise formulation, which is crucial for reproducibility. The text later uses DS · f (Algorithm 1) which seems to denote the same thing. This inconsistency in notation (f[smoothed] vs. DS · f) is minor but could confuse careful readers.

comments.title: Ambiguity in perturbation size epsilon for adversarial examples
comments.quote: We use the SMOOTHADVPGD method in Salman et al. (2019) and sample Monte-Carlo noise vectors to estimate the gradients of the _smoothed_ classifier. Adversarial examples are generated with a _l_ 2 norm bound _ϵ_.
comments.explanation: The text states that adversarial examples are generated with an l2 norm bound ε, but it does not specify what value(s) of ε are used across experiments. In Section 4.2, examples are shown for ε=20 and ε=60, but these seem specific to the illustrative binary classifier. Later in Section 5.1, ε=100 is used for a camouflaged backdoor. The methodology section (Algorithm 1) does not prescribe how to choose ε, leaving it to the practitioner. While this is common in adversarial example literature, the effectiveness of the method likely depends on picking a sufficiently large ε to reveal perceptible patterns. Not providing guidance on ε selection could hinder reproducibility. However, given that the paper is under review and may have more details in an appendix, this is a moderate underspecification issue.

comments.title: Unjustified claim about 'distribution of triggers' from Qiao et al. (2019)
comments.quote: This work provides more empirical evidence that poisoning a classifier does not inject one specific backdoor, but also many possible effective triggers.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that their results provide 'more empirical evidence' for the existence of a distribution of triggers, citing Qiao et al. (2019). However, the provided text does not show any formal modeling or quantification of such a distribution. The paper demonstrates that alternative triggers can be constructed from adversarial examples, but this does not necessarily imply a 'distribution' in a statistical or probabilistic sense. The claim is vague and could overstate what has been shown. A careful reader might expect some parametric or non-parametric characterization of trigger variability, which is absent. This is a minor overclaim.

comments.title: Potential contradiction in 'untargeted' adversarial example use
comments.quote: Throughout this paper, unless otherwise specified, we generate _untargeted_ adversarial examples (though through these untargeted examples it will become obvious which is the poisoned class).
comments.explanation: The authors state they use untargeted adversarial examples to discover backdoor patterns. However, the procedure described later (Algorithm 1) minimizes the loss L(DS·f(x*), y) which is a standard formulation for targeted or untargeted attacks depending on the loss function. In untargeted attacks, one typically maximizes the loss (or equivalently, minimizes the negative log-likelihood of the true class). The text says they 'minimize' the loss, which is ambiguous. If they minimize the standard cross-entropy loss, that would actually produce an image closer to the true class, not an adversarial example. This is almost certainly a typo: they likely mean maximize or use a targeted attack loss. The inconsistency could mislead a reader trying to reproduce the method. Given the context of generating adversarial examples, it's likely an error in describing the optimization direction.

comments.title: Ambiguity in human selection criteria for alternative triggers
comments.quote: We select the patches which have dense distinctive
comments.explanation: The sentence is cut off (likely due to OCR), but the preceding text describes selecting 'regional backdoor patterns with human inspection' and 'patches which have dense distinctive...'. The criteria for what constitutes a 'suspicious' region is not formally defined. While this is a human-in-the-loop method, for reproducibility, a more concrete description of what visual features (e.g., color saturation, spatial coherence, contrast with background) guide the selection would be helpful. The paper acknowledges this limitation (Section 4.3), but the missing text leaves the criteria ambiguous. This is a minor underspecification.

comments.title: Misleading presentation of attack success rate for clean classifiers
comments.quote: To measure the effect of the triggers constructed by our procedure on clean classifiers, we report the error rate of clean classifiers when the test data is patched by the alternative triggers.
comments.explanation: The authors use 'error rate' for clean classifiers, but for poisoned classifiers they use 'attack success rate' (ASR). These are not directly comparable. For a clean classifier, patching an image with a trigger should ideally not change the predicted class (error rate low), while for a poisoned classifier, the goal is to force misclassification to target class (ASR high). However, the text states 'our attack does not apply to clean classifiers' based on low error rates. This is logically sound, but the phrasing 'error rate' is ambiguous: it could mean the classifier's original error on clean data (which is low) or the error induced by the patch. From context, they mean the fraction of patched images that are misclassified (any misclassification), which is appropriate. Clarifying this would avoid confusion.
